metadata.title: AQA GCSE Biology (8461)
metadata.summary: Candidate package rebuilt from official AQA specification pages, official AQA command-word guidance, and June 2023 examiner reports.

sources.title: AQA GCSE Biology 8461/1H Report on the Examination June 2023
AQA GCSE Biology 8461/2H Report on the Examination June 2023

derivedFrom.note: Legacy seed at src/data/seeds/gcse-biology-aqa.json used only as a regression artifact and cross-check.

components.name: Paper 1: Cell biology; Organisation; Infection and response; Bioenergetics
Paper 2: Homeostasis and response; Inheritance, variation and evolution; Ecology

topics.name: Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Coronary heart disease: a non-communicable disease
The effect of lifestyle on some non-communicable diseases
Detection and identification of plant diseases
Maintaining water and nitrogen balance in the body (biology only)
The use of hormones to treat infertility (HT only)
Advantages and disadvantages of sexual and asexual reproduction (biology only)
The development of understanding of genetics and evolution
Impact of environmental change (biology only) (HT only)
Biodiversity and the effect of human interaction on ecosystems
Trophic levels in an ecosystem (biology only)

edges.rationale: Cell division assumes secure cell-structure knowledge.
Membrane transport depends on cell structure and membranes.
Animal organ systems build on the cell-tissue-organ hierarchy.
Plant systems build on the same organisation model.
Vaccination is taught within the communicable-disease model.
Antibiotic use and resistance build on bacterial disease knowledge.
Coordination topics extend the core homeostasis idea.
Hormonal control is another homeostatic control pathway.
Variation and evolution rely on prior reproduction and meiosis content.
Genetics-and-evolution development builds on inherited variation.
Ecosystem organisation assumes prior adaptation and community knowledge.
Human-impact topics depend on the ecosystem model.
Food production decisions build on biodiversity and human-impact trade-offs.

commandWords.definition: Students need to balance a chemical equation.
Students should use numbers given in the question to work out the answer.
Select from a range of alternatives.
This requires the student to describe the similarities and/or differences between things, not just write about one.
commandWords.guidance: Write paired similarities or differences rather than isolated facts about one side.

commandWords.definition: Answers should be written in the space provided, for example, on a diagram, in spaces in a sentence or in a table.
Specify the meaning of something.
Students may be asked to recall some facts, events or process in an accurate way.
Set out how something will be done.
Use given data or information to obtain and answer.
To produce, or add to, a diagram.
Assign an approximate value.
Students should use the information supplied, as well as their knowledge and understanding, to consider evidence for and against when making a judgement.
commandWords.guidance: Use the evidence provided, weigh both sides, then reach a justified judgement.

commandWords.definition: Students should make something clear, or state the reasons for something happening.
commandWords.guidance: State the biological mechanism and link it to the resulting outcome.

commandWords.definition: Only a short answer is required, not an explanation or a description.
These can be used for more direct questions.
Name or otherwise characterise.
Use evidence from the information supplied to support an answer.
commandWords.guidance: Support the choice or conclusion with evidence from the question or data provided.

commandWords.definition: Provide appropriate names on a diagram.
Find an item of data for a given quantity.
Only a short answer is required, not an explanation or a description. Often it can be answered with a single word, phrase or sentence.
Write a method.
Mark on a graph using data given.
Give a plausible outcome.
Provide structured evidence to reach a conclusion.
Draw approximately.
This term is used in questions where students need to apply their knowledge and understanding to a new situation.
commandWords.guidance: Apply existing knowledge to the unfamiliar context instead of recalling a memorised sentence.

commandWords.definition: The answer must be based on the information given in the question. Unless the information given in the question is used, no marks can be given. In some cases students might be asked to use their own knowledge and understanding.
commandWords.guidance: Reference the given data or information explicitly or the answer will not secure credit.

commandWords.definition: Only a short answer is required, not an explanation or a description.

questionTypes.name: Multiple choice
questionTypes.description: Objective single-best-answer questions listed in the specification at a glance question format.

questionTypes.name: Structured
questionTypes.description: Multi-step structured items that require ordered scientific reasoning and method/application detail.

questionTypes.name: Closed short answer
questionTypes.description: Short-response items with tightly delimited factual, procedural, or data-reading answers.

questionTypes.name: Open response
questionTypes.description: Extended written responses that reward linked biological reasoning and judgement.

misconceptionRules.description: Treats osmosis as antibiotic molecules entering bacteria rather than water moving across a partially permeable membrane.
triggerPatterns: osmosis means chemicals move into bacteria
osmosis moves the antibiotic into the cell
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Osmosis is the net movement of water through a partially permeable membrane from higher water potential to lower water potential.

misconceptionRules.description: Treats a tissue as any group of cells and omits the idea that the cells are similar in structure or function.
triggerPatterns: tissue is a group of cells
any cells working together make a tissue
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: A tissue is a group of similar cells organised to carry out a particular shared function.

misconceptionRules.description: Confuses the role of the coronary arteries with vessels that carry blood to the lungs or around the rest of the body.
triggerPatterns: coronary arteries take blood to the lungs
coronary arteries move blood around the body
blocked coronary artery stops blood reaching the lungs
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Coronary arteries supply oxygenated blood to the heart muscle itself, so blockage reduces aerobic respiration in cardiac muscle.

misconceptionRules.description: Knows that toxins matter in salmonella infection but does not state that the bacteria release or secrete them.
triggerPatterns: toxin inside the bacteria causes diarrhoea
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Salmonella bacteria produce and release toxins, and those toxins damage the body and cause symptoms such as diarrhoea and vomiting.

misconceptionRules.description: Mixes up antigens, antibodies, antitoxins and memory cells when explaining vaccination.
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Vaccination introduces antigens, stimulates antibody production, and leaves memory cells that speed up the secondary response on re-exposure.

misconceptionRules.description: Thinks plants respire only at night rather than continuously.
triggerPatterns: respiration happens only when photosynthesis stops
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Plants respire all the time; photosynthesis happens only in light, but respiration continues day and night.

misconceptionRules.description: Describes the lens as larger or more concave when focusing on a near object.
triggerPatterns: lens gets larger for near focus
lens becomes concave to see near objects
lens stretches flatter for nearby objects
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: To focus on a near object the lens becomes thicker, rounder and more convex.

misconceptionRules.description: Has weak or fragmented understanding of kidney function and the role of the kidney in homeostasis.
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: The kidneys filter the blood, selectively reabsorb useful substances and water, and regulate water and ion balance while urea is excreted in urine.

misconceptionRules.description: Treats tissue culture as simply using tissue instead of growing cells to produce clones.
triggerPatterns: tissue culture means using plant tissue
tissue culture is cutting up tissue only
tissue culture means growing tissue without cells
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Tissue culture grows plant cells or small tissue samples by mitosis to produce many genetically identical clones.

misconceptionRules.description: Confuses meiosis with mitosis or cannot use meiosis to explain genetic variation.
triggerPatterns: meiosis is for growth and repair
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Meiosis halves the chromosome number, produces genetically different gametes, and underpins variation in sexual reproduction.

misconceptionRules.description: Says bacteria become immune to antibiotics instead of resistant to them.
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: Random mutations can make some bacteria resistant to an antibiotic, and selection increases the frequency of resistant strains.

misconceptionRules.description: Uses ecosystem when the question is asking for the community of organisms living together in a habitat.
triggerPatterns: all organisms in a habitat make an ecosystem only
misconceptionRules.correctionGuidance: A community is all the populations of different species living together in a habitat, while an ecosystem also includes the abiotic environment.

taskRules.title: Build coronary-artery explanations as a causal chain
taskRules.guidance: Rehearse the chain blocked coronary artery -> less oxygen to heart muscle -> less aerobic respiration -> less energy released -> reduced pumping efficiency / fatigue / heart attack risk.

taskRules.title: Sequence vaccination responses explicitly
taskRules.guidance: Make students state antigen, antibody, memory cell, and faster secondary response in order before attempting prose answers about vaccination.

taskRules.title: Separate resistance from immunity
taskRules.guidance: Use short retrieval drills that contrast resistant bacteria, immune individuals, and the selective pressure produced by antibiotic use.

taskRules.title: Model osmosis with water-potential language
taskRules.guidance: Use worked examples that force learners to name water as the moving substance, identify the partially permeable membrane, and state the direction of net movement.

taskRules.title: Rehearse accommodation as muscle-lens-pupil changes
taskRules.guidance: Model near and bright-light responses as linked changes in ciliary muscle, lens shape, iris muscles and pupil size, using precise anatomical vocabulary.

taskRules.title: Re-enter kidney questions through the blood-filter-reabsorb-excrete sequence
taskRules.guidance: If kidney questions are going badly, route the learner back through the ordered sequence blood filtration -> selective reabsorption -> urine formation before asking for detail.

taskRules.title: Contrast meiosis with mitosis before genetics problems
taskRules.guidance: Use short retrieval prompts that force chromosome number, number of divisions, cell type produced and genetic similarity for both meiosis and mitosis.

taskRules.title: State that plants respire continuously before discussing day-night differences
taskRules.guidance: Require the learner to say 'plants respire all the time' before adding what changes between day and night in photosynthesis and gas exchange.

taskRules.title: Lock down control variables and sample-size reasoning in breeding investigations
taskRules.guidance: Review answers that use vague environment wording and replace them with named control variables plus a clear reason why larger sample sizes improve representativeness and reduce anomaly effects.
